summary: Jeff Atwood examines whether software certifications are meaningful credentials for developers. He acknowledges a tension: while certifications might establish baseline competency for junior developers, he's seen certified senior developers who still lacked fundamental skills. Citing Tom DeMarco's 1998 critique that certification is really about power seizure by certifiers rather than societal benefit, Atwood ultimately sides with letting the market determine competence. His conclusion is that portfolios and demonstrated work matter far more than passing standardized tests, though certifications aren't inherently harmful if accompanied by real accomplishments.
key_points: Certifications are often sold by the same companies that make the products, creating an inherent conflict of interest
At junior levels, certifications might ensure baseline knowledge of fundamental concepts
Senior developers with multiple certifications can still be incompetent, proving certifications don't guarantee skill
Tom DeMarco argues certification is about power seizure by certifiers, not societal benefit
The market is a more competent and ethical selection mechanism than any appointed certification elite
money_quotes: That's nice, but show me what you've worked on.
Your credentials should be the sum of the projects you've worked on, and specifically how much you learned from your failures.
You should be spending your time building stuff, not studying for multiple choice tests.
Though the rationale for certification is always societal good, the real objective is different: seizure of power.
The real issue here is not certification; the real issue is de-certification.
Sorry about that Mr. Gates, in the brave new world, you wouldn't be allowed to write software.
I suggest that we have a perfectly fine selection mechanism at work today; it's called the market.
key_insight: A developer's portfolio of completed projects and lessons learned from failures is far more valuable than any certification alphabet soup.
